questions.passage: The sundial survives mostly as decoration, a garden ornament engraved with a proverb about time's flight. It is easy to treat it as a quaint failure: a clock that works only when the sun cooperates, cannot be worn on the wrist, and refuses the precision required by trains, factories, and calendar alerts. But this judgment assumes that the only purpose of a timekeeping device is to deliver time instantly and accurately. The sundial asks for a different relation. It tells time by requiring the observer to notice light, season, latitude, and weather. Mechanical and digital clocks abstract time from place. That abstraction has enormous social value. Shared schedules allow surgeries, school days, court hearings, and flights to happen. A nostalgia for purely local time would be foolish and, for many forms of coordination, unjust. The person waiting for a bus does not need a meditation on shadow; she needs a service to arrive when promised. Still, the gain in precision can conceal a loss in attention. Digital time appears everywhere and nowhere. It floats at the top of screens, identical in hotel rooms and kitchens, attached to tasks rather than skies. The sundial, by contrast, cannot be consulted without acknowledging dependence on conditions outside the self. Clouds do not make it inaccurate in the way a broken clock is inaccurate; they remind the user that measurement was always an agreement with the world. This is why sundials often attract moral inscriptions. The sayings may be sentimental, but the sentiment arises from the instrument's form. To read a sundial is to see time as a relation among body, light, and surface. Its slowness is not efficient, and efficiency matters. But efficiency is not the only intellectual virtue. Sometimes a device teaches by refusing to disappear into usefulness. The point is not that modern people should abandon precise clocks for garden shadows. Rather, the sundial exposes how much our usual instruments omit in order to serve us. It offers no practical replacement for the schedule. It offers a reminder that time is not only counted; it is also encountered. The attraction of the sundial is therefore not that it tells better time. It plainly does not. Its value lies in the inconvenience by which it reminds the observer that measurement is also a choice of attention. Digital time travels everywhere unchanged, and that portability lets modern life coordinate itself at scales no village could manage. But portability can become forgetfulness. Noon on a phone is the same in a windowless hallway as in a field; noon on a dial depends on exposure, direction, and shadow. To notice this dependence is not to reject modern timekeeping. It is to recognize that accuracy often requires stripping away circumstance. The sundial's lesson is not anti-modern. It is a warning that tools praised for efficiency may train users to ignore the conditions that make their abstractions possible. This is why the sundial can be instructive even when no one depends on it for punctuality. It slows the act of reading time until the observer notices that a number on a screen is the end of a long chain of relations. The shadow has length because the earth turns; it shifts because the season changes; it disappears because clouds intervene. Modern clocks spare us from attending to these relations, and for many tasks that mercy is indispensable. The question is whether convenience should also erase the memory that time once had to be read from the world before it could be carried in a pocket.
questions.stem: The passage’s central argument about sundials is that they:

choices.A: should replace digital clocks in settings where attention matters.
choices.B: are valuable because they provide more accurate time than modern devices.
choices.C: reveal what efficient timekeeping omits from attention.
choices.D: show that shared schedules are morally corrupting.

explanations.A: The author calls replacement foolish for many purposes. The sundial is not offered as a practical substitute.
explanations.B: The passage openly admits that sundials are less precise and less convenient.
explanations.C: Correct. The sundial matters because it requires noticing light, season, direction, and place. It exposes the circumstances abstracted away by portable time.
explanations.D: Shared schedules are described as socially valuable and sometimes necessary for justice.

questions.stem: The reference to buses, surgeries, and court hearings primarily serves to:

choices.A: prove that old timekeeping devices have no remaining value.
choices.B: show that precision matters only in government institutions.
choices.C: mock modern people for caring about punctuality.
choices.D: acknowledge the social importance of abstract clock time.

explanations.A: The passage preserves value for the sundial after admitting modern coordination matters.
explanations.B: The examples are varied and illustrative, not a limit on where precision matters.
explanations.C: The tone is not mocking. The author treats punctual services as real needs.
explanations.D: Correct. These examples prevent the argument from becoming nostalgia. They show why standardized time is indispensable.

questions.stem: A school installs a sundial beside digital clocks and uses it to teach students about shadows and seasons. The author would likely view this as:

choices.A: misguided because students should learn to distrust clock time entirely.
choices.B: pointless because the sundial cannot coordinate the school day.
choices.C: valuable only if the digital clocks are removed.
choices.D: a fitting use because it supplements precision with situated attention.

explanations.A: The author does not urge distrust of clock time. The passage values modern coordination while noting what it conceals.
explanations.B: The sundial need not coordinate the day to have educational value. Its value is attentional.
explanations.C: The passage rejects the replacement frame. The two devices can serve different purposes.
explanations.D: Correct. The installation uses the sundial exactly as the passage values it: not as a replacement for schedules, but as a reminder that measurement depends on conditions.

questions.stem: The author’s attitude toward digital time is best described as:

choices.A: hostile because it detaches people from every meaningful experience.
choices.B: uncritically admiring because it enables large-scale coordination.
choices.C: grateful for its usefulness but alert to its abstractions.
choices.D: indifferent because timekeeping devices are only decorative.

explanations.A: The passage acknowledges losses, but does not treat digital time as wholly destructive.
explanations.B: The author admires its coordination while insisting that abstraction can produce forgetfulness.
explanations.C: Correct. This captures the balanced stance: modern timekeeping is useful and sometimes just, but it strips away circumstance.
explanations.D: Devices are not merely decorative in the passage. Their forms train attention.

questions.stem: In context, “portability can become forgetfulness” means that:

choices.A: portable clocks often fail more frequently than fixed instruments.
choices.B: portable time may hide its dependence on place and conditions.
choices.C: mobile devices make people nostalgic for preindustrial villages.
choices.D: travelers forget appointments when they rely on phones.

explanations.A: Reliability is not the issue. The passage is concerned with what portable time leaves unnoticed.
explanations.B: Correct. The phrase points to the way portable time can make noon seem detached from light, weather, latitude, and exposure.
explanations.C: The author explicitly avoids nostalgia for purely local time.
explanations.D: Missed appointments are not discussed. The concern is conceptual rather than practical failure.

questions.stem: Which finding would most strengthen the passage?

choices.A: People generally prefer digital clocks when catching trains.
choices.B: Sundial use makes students more attentive to seasonal daylight.
choices.C: Garden sundials are often purchased for decorative reasons.
choices.D: Most smartphones automatically update when users cross time zones.

explanations.A: The author already grants practical preference for precise scheduling; this does not support the deeper attentional claim.
explanations.B: Correct. This supports the central claim that sundials train attention to conditions abstracted by modern clocks.
explanations.C: Decoration is the starting stereotype the passage moves beyond.
explanations.D: Automatic updating illustrates portability, but does not by itself show the attentional loss the author identifies.

questions.stem: The author would most likely disagree with which statement?

choices.A: A device is valuable only if it works better than alternatives.
choices.B: Efficiency can be gained by removing details from experience.
choices.C: An obsolete tool may still clarify the assumptions of a useful modern one.
choices.D: Attention to place can coexist with reliance on standardized schedules.

explanations.A: Correct. The passage’s defense of the sundial depends on rejecting this standard. It is valuable despite being worse as a clock.
explanations.B: This aligns with the passage’s claim that abstraction serves efficiency by omitting circumstance.
explanations.C: The sundial is valuable in precisely this way.
explanations.D: The author’s position is not either-or. Modern schedules and situated attention can coexist.
